data.title: Watch live: Special coverage of King Charles III joint meeting with Congress
data.description:
Nexstar’s Basil John will be hosting wraparound coverage of King Charles III’s speech to Congress on Tuesday, joined by The Hill’s Sarakshi Rai and Nexstar’s Vinay Simlot.

Coverage will start at 2:30 p.m. EDT and is expected to run until 4 p.m. EDT.

King Charles’s speech expected to begin at 3p.m. EDT.

The royal tour in the U.S. marks Charles’s first trip to the U.S. as monarch. 

Watch the live video above.

data.title: House Democrat on new Trump ballroom push: 'No one gives a s—'
data.description:
Rep. Rob Menendez (D-N.J.) on Monday criticized a push from some Republicans to use taxpayer money to fund the construction of the White House ballroom, slamming his counterparts for “doing whatever they can to appease” President Trump.

“They’re so misaligned with what the American people need and what they want, right?” he told reporter Pablo Manríquez outside the Capitol. “There’s an affordability crisis out there. No one gives a s— about the president’s ballroom except the president, right? That’s the reality.”

A group of Senate Republicans led by Sen. Lindsey Graham (R-S.C.) is floating a bill to authorize up to $400 million for the construction of a 90,000-square-foot ballroom on the site of the former East Wing. They said national park user fees and customs fees could be used to offset the cost.

The massive project was set to be funded mostly by private donors.

Trump has often pointed to the ballroom as a necessary addition so that future administrations can hold large state events indoors, rather than continuing to use outdoor tents.

But he has also argued it is vital to national security, referencing multiple security upgrades expected to coincide with the project, including the overhaul of a military bunker buried deep beneath the building.

“I didn’t want to say this, but this is why we have to have all of the attributes of what we’re planning at the White House,” Trump told reporters in the briefing room on Saturday night after a gunman rushed security and opened fire at the White House Correspondents’ Association (WHCA) dinner.  

“It’s drone-proof. It’s bulletproof glass. We need the ballroom. That’s why Secret Service, that’s why the military are demanding it,” the president continued.  “They wanted the ballroom for 150 years for lots of different reasons, but today is a little bit different because today we need levels of security that nobody has ever seen before.”

No one was injured in the attack at the Washington Hilton hotel, which authorities said targeted administration officials. The suspect, 31-year-old Cole Allen, was charged with attempting to assassinate the president and other firearm-related offenses.

Renewed calls for the ballroom to be built quickly followed on Saturday, with Democratic Sen. John Fetterman (Penn.) urging his colleagues in a post online to “drop the TDS” and support its construction.

“We were there front and center. That venue wasn’t built to accommodate an event with the line of succession for the U.S. government. After witnessing last night, drop the TDS and build the White House ballroom for events exactly like these,” Fetterman wrote, referring to “Trump derangement syndrome,” a phrase coined by the president.

The ballroom was slated for completion by 2028, but a federal judge halted construction last month after finding Trump did not have the authority to build it without congressional approval.

The Justice Department is pressing the National Trust for Historic Preservation in the United States to drop its lawsuit in the wake of the WHCA dinner shooting, but the group has so far declined.

data.title: Watch live: House Democrats give remarks as redistricting battle ramps up
data.description:
House Democratic Caucus Chair Pete Aguilar (Calif.) and other top Democrats will give remarks Tuesday morning amid the redistricting battle.

Last week’s victory in Virginia, when voters approved a redistricting referendum that favors Democrats, added new momentum for the party ahead of the midterms. The updated district lines, however, are facing some legal scrutiny.

Florida Gov. Ron DeSantis (R) is seeking to erase that movement with a new map for the Sunshine State. DeSantis and House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries have traded barbs in recent days over the issue.

The press conference also comes as the administration casts blame on Democrats’ rhetoric for a shooting at the White House Correspondents’ Association dinner over the weekend. The suspect, Cole Allen, has been charged with attempted assassination of President Trump and multiple firearms offenses.

The event is scheduled to begin at 10:45 a.m. EDT.

Watch the live video above.

data.title: Watch live: McMahon faces questions over Education budget
data.description:
Education Secretary Linda McMahon will testify before a Senate Appropriations Committee panel Tuesday morning on President Trump’s fiscal 2027 budget request for the mostly shuttered Education Department.

The department has been on the chopping block since Trump returned to the Oval Office last year. The administration has shifted many of its programs to other federal agencies including the Small Business Administration as well as the Health and Human Services and State departments in an effort to “to break up the federal education bureaucracy and return education to the states.”

The latest budget requests $76.5 billion for fiscal 2027, a $2.3 billion decrease from the 2026 enacted level.

The event is scheduled to begin at 10 a.m. EDT.

Watch the live video above.

data.title: House in limbo as GOP struggles to find path for spy powers, Farm Bill, ICE funding
data.description:
House Republicans’ ambitious agenda for the week is in limbo as leaders struggle to come to a consensus with hardline conservatives on legislation to reauthorize foreign spy powers, the Farm Bill, and a party-line budget blueprint to fund immigration enforcement as part of a two-step plan to end the record-long Department of Homeland Security (DHS) shutdown. 

The House Rules Committee met on Monday to advance a single procedural rule that, if it passed on the floor, would tee up all three major pieces of legislation. But it went into recess overnight as Republicans who are demanding various policy additions and leaders tried to hash out agreements.

The Rules panel initially said it would return to session at 8 a.m. on Tuesday, but shortly after, the panel again indefinitely postponed its return time.

“The consideration of the aforementioned measures has been postponed subject to the call of the chair,” the Rules Committee said on X.

That leaves the House, which had been aiming to advance the procedural rule and hold a final vote on the spy powers bill on Tuesday, scrambling for a solution as Republicans butt heads on the policy details.

Republicans need near-unanimity to advance the procedural rule to tee up consideration of the underlying bills, since Democrats typically do not help with rule votes, which are tests of the majority’s control of the House floor.

It’s unclear when the committee will reconvene. Further complicating the schedule is a Tuesday afternoon joint meeting for an address from King Charles, which is set for 3 p.m.

Rep. Chip Roy (R-Texas), policy chair of the House Freedom Caucus and a member of the Rules Committee, forecasted those problems while exiting a GOP conference meeting Monday night.

“We’d like to see more reforms on FISA. We’ll see what happens. I haven’t drawn any red lines, but I certainly like to see some improvements to that, improvements to DHS funding, and improvements to farm bill on a number of different levels. So we’ll see what happens in the next 24 hours,” Roy said.

Reauthorization of Section 702 of the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act (FISA), which authorizes surveillance of foreign individuals overseas and expires on Thursday, is at the top of Republicans’ priority list. But FISA has given House GOP leaders headaches for weeks. Republicans seeking reforms to the program resisted President Trump’s call to pass a “clean” reauthorization, leading to dramatic late-night offer from GOP leaders that holdouts promptly rejected in a late-night vote earlier this month.

Republicans last week unveiled another FISA compromise with many of those same parameters, which they tried to tee up to pass on Tuesday.

Under the latest legislation, Section 702 would be reauthorized for three years, and while it references the Fourth Amendment, it does not require a warrant before looking at information swept up on Americans communicating with foreigners being surveilled. It’s a hard line for many lawmakers on both the left and the right.

“They need to gut FISA and come back with a different product, I think, change the name, go back to what it originally was supposed to do, to spy on folks that are non-Americans and provide some clarification that it won’t be used to spy on us,” Rep. Tim Burchett (R-Tenn.) said.

Also at issue is the Farm Bill, which is facing a rebellion from Republicans aligned with the Make America Healthy Again (MAHA) movement. Among the biggest issue are provisions relating to pesticides, which opponents say would shield pesticide makers from liability and strip protections to keep pesticides out of drinking water.

Rep. Anna Paulina Luna (R-Fla.) said on X that she and others “will slaughter the Farm Bill” unless sections relating to pesticides are removed.

On top of those major pieces of legislation, Republicans are still battling over the mechanics of a GOP-only budget reconciliation blueprint to fund immigration and border enforcement.

The Senate last week passed a “skinny” budget blueprint to fund Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) and Border Patrol through the reconciliation process — which bypasses the threat of a Democratic filibuster as the Democrats demand significant reforms as a condition of funding.

Congress is racing the clock. GOP leaders in the House have insisted on passing the reconciliation bill to fund immigration enforcement before taking up a bill to fund the rest of DHS — even as officials warn the money they’ve been using to pay employees is about to run out.

But the budget plan has gotten grumbles from Republicans who worry about losing another shot at a party-line reconciliation bill, and are hoping for assurances for another bill later in the year.

“I think there was a lot of talk tonight from the leadership about reconciliation 3.0, and as I mentioned before, in order for people to get comfortable with just running a skinny 2.0, there needs to be a sense of confidence and in the commitment from our conference leaders that we will go for a third one, because there still remains a number of policy reforms to advance that the American people sent us up here to do,” House Budget Committee Chair Jodey Arrington (R-Texas) said Monday after the conference meeting.

Rebecca Beitsch contributed.

data.title: The great bait-and-switch of state assisted suicide laws
data.description:
Last March, 31-year-old Eileen Mihich was found dead in a room at the Hotel Deluxe in Portland, Ore. Near her body was an empty bottle of the poison pills prescribed specifically for physician-assisted suicide.

Mihich had obtained this concoction from a pharmacy in Mill Creek, Wash., despite not meeting any of the legal criteria to obtain it. She was not terminally ill. She had not seen even one practitioner. She also had serious mental illness that rendered her capacity questionable, and she was not a resident of Washington state.

One year after lodging a formal complaint with the Washington Department of Health, her family has still received no word on how their loved one could have received these deadly drugs.

The incident helps illustrate the classic bait-and-switch nature of the modern assisted suicide movement and the effort to make suicide-affirmation a form of medical care.

The first U.S. state to legalize the practice, Oregon, promised that cases like that of Mihich wouldn’t happen. In the subsequent decades, the assisted suicide lobby has continuously protested too much that no documented case of abuse exists. This nonsense has long been discredited. In fact, the very first woman to die from Oregon’s original suicide law was pushed through the doctor-shopping process despite her likely ineligibility. A litany of other cases of abuse has followed.

Outside the U.S., it is almost satirical how ravenous the system has become for new victims of abusive practices. Recently, multiple outlets have documented how an 81-year-old woman in Canada was offered to be put to death by the state on account of her back pain.

Mihich’s tragic case ought to bring renewed scrutiny to how this flimsy, dangerous system operates in the U.S. Bills to legalize the practice are active in 13 states, and two other states are looking at removing their existing safeguards.

Eileen’s workaround was disturbingly simple and works in all 13 states where suicide-affirming care is legal. She knew she did not meet her native Oregon’s eligibility requirements. So she found a random doctor’s publicly available National Provider Identifier, which she used to forge a prescription. She spoofed that doctor’s email address and submitted the fraudulent application by email to a pharmacy, which signed the prescription.

Eileen picked up the fatal dose shortly thereafter.

An exposé by The Atlantic clearly demonstrates that the pharmacy in question violated Washington law, which states that physician requests for suicide drugs can only be sent by electronic medical record, fax, mail, or hand delivery. Eileen coyly lamented that her fax machine was broken and asked if she could send the form by email — “Okay,” is all the pharmacy texted back.

But in truth, the law still would have failed even if Eileen had fulfilled this requirement by simply mailing the fake prescription or faxing it from her local FedEx store. Her copycat Gmail account, her bogus personal residence address, the spoofed doctor’s office address, and a real National Provider Identifier number all would have evaded detection by lax pharmacists.

Accountability for abuse is anathema to state suicide protocols. Although morbidity and mortality evaluations are routine in hospitals, the prescription of lethal poisons for patients is an unpoliced free-for-all. Practitioners are required to make reports, yet hundreds are missing every year. Washington had nearly 200 missing physician forms and 75 missing patient consent forms in 2024 alone. We don’t know if those patients voluntarily requested death, were of sound mind, or had a terminal diagnosis. And people suffering from treatable mental illnesses, like Mihich, simply don’t get psychiatric screenings. Less than 1 percent of cases receive such interventions.

This lack of accountability, combined with weak safeguards, is deadly. And in Washington, the Department of Health has ceased producing the legally required public reports. New Mexico and Montana have never produced any data. Hawaii has downgraded its reporting standards, and our nation’s capital, Washington, D.C., has failed to produce an annual report in several years.

Eileen is a victim of the decades-long bait-and-switch operation from the suicide-affirming care lobby. They promised us guardrails, transparency, and accountability; we’re still waiting for transparency and accountability, and the longer we wait, the more guardrails are torn down.

While Eileen’s family cries over their irreversible loss, suicide pill-mills claim more lives. Her death is the inevitable consequence of legislating entire classes of individuals as worthy of suicide.

Eileen deserved help; instead, the state served her a death sentence. The loopholes exploited by this poor, desperate woman must be tightened. If we could not help Eileen in her earthly life, we can honor her memory now through a moratorium on further expansions of assisted suicide laws, and with actual accountability for those who caused her death from Washington’s Department of Health.

Jamie Towey is president of Aging with Dignity.

data.title: UAE announces plans to leave OPEC: State media
data.description:
The United Arab Emirates (UAE) will leave the Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries (OPEC), the country’s government said on Tuesday. 

The country will officially exit OPEC and its broader OPEC+ association on May 1 following the government’s internal review of its production policy, according to UAE state media, WAM. 

“This decision reflects the UAE’s long-term strategic and economic vision and evolving energy profile, including accelerated investment in domestic energy production, and reinforces its commitment to a responsible, reliable, and forward-looking role in global energy markets,” WAM reported. 

The move marks a major change for the permanent intergovernmental organization, which sets uniform petroleum policies among its member countries with the mission of maintaining stable global oil markets.

The UAE first joined OPEC seven years after its founding in 1960 under the emirate of Abu Dhabi, and it has been a vital member of the intergovernmental organization. The country’s exit follows UAE officials’ complaints about OPEC’s quotas and the country’s reported clashes with the organization’s de-facto leader, Saudi Arabia. 

The UAE’s government said it still plans to collaborate with other petroleum-exporting nations to promote joint economic growth and market stability, according to the Tuesday report. 

“This decision reflects the UAE’s long-term strategic and economic vision and evolving energy profile, including accelerated investment in domestic energy production, and reinforces its commitment to a responsible, reliable, and forward-looking role in global energy markets,” the state media outlet reported.

data.title: Clooney defends Kimmel: 'Jokes are jokes'
data.description:
George Clooney is defending Jimmy Kimmel after President Trump and Melania Trump called for the late-night show host’s firing over a joke he made ahead of the White House Correspondents’ Association (WHCA) dinner about the first lady looking like an “expectant widow.”

“Jimmy’s a comedian,” the “Good Night, and Good Luck” star told Variety at the Chaplin Award Gala in New York on Monday.

“I look at that side and go ‘Jokes are jokes,’ but the rhetoric I think is a little dangerous and we’ve seen it a lot lately,” Clooney said.

In a sketch on ABC’s “Jimmy Kimmel Live!” that aired two days before the WHCA dinner, Kimmel said that Melania Trump had the “glow” of “an expectant widow.”

A suspected shooter at the Correspondents’ dinner was later charged with attempting to assassinate Trump, who was attending the annual event for the first time as president on Saturday at the Washington Hilton hotel. 

Trump on Monday described Kimmel’s remark as a “despicable call to violence,” saying the comedian should be fired by Disney, ABC’s parent company. Hours earlier, Melania Trump posted on social media that Kimmel’s comments were “hateful and violent rhetoric” that were “intended to divide our country.”

Kimmel later defended the quip, saying it was about the more than 20-year age gap between the president and his wife.

“It was a very light roast joke about the fact that he’s almost 80 and she’s younger than I am,” he said.

Last year, Kimmel was briefly suspended from his late-night show following pressure from the Trump administration over jokes he made following the assassination of conservative activist Charlie Kirk. 

In his interview this week with Variety, Clooney compared Kimmel’s “expectant widow” remark to comments White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt gave on the red carpet ahead of the WHCA dinner at the Washington Hilton hotel. 

“He is ready to rumble,” Leavitt said of Trump. “This speech tonight will be classic Donald J. Trump. It will be funny. It’ll be entertaining. There will be some shots fired tonight in the room. So everyone should tune in.”

“I would argue that Karoline Leavitt didn’t mean that shots should be fired. Right? She was making a joke. Fair enough.”

Asked if some “unity” between the press and the Trump administration might happen in the future, the 64-year-old Academy Award winner replied, “I always have faith in the press. I always believe in it.”

“I’m the son of a journalist,” Clooney, whose father Nick was a longtime TV news anchor, said.

“I also think that you’re not supposed to have a good relationship with government. You’re supposed to be questioning them,” Clooney said.

“I’m a Democrat, I make no apologies for it. The people that I support — [former Presidents] Bill Clinton, Barack Obama — they didn’t enjoy getting chased down by reporters. People in power don’t like to have to answer all the questions in the world, that’s fair.”

“They shouldn’t enjoy it and the reporters should make sure they hold people’s feet to the fire,” Clooney said. 

“That’s the rules.”

data.title: Trump pulls Park Service nominee
data.description:
President Trump on Monday withdrew his nominee to lead the National Park Service (NPS).

The White House announced the withdrawal of hospitality executive Scott Socha from consideration amid a slate of other nominations and withdrawals.

The reason for the withdrawal was not immediately clear.

Trump nominated Socha, president of parks and resorts at Delaware North, in February.

The NPS position requires Senate confirmation.

data.title: John Hinckley: Washington Hilton isn't safe
data.description:
John Hinckley Jr., the failed assassin who shot former President Reagan outside the Washington Hilton in 1981, on Sunday said the hotel is not safe, after a shooting during the White House Correspondents’ Association dinner.

On Saturday, an armed man charged through a security checkpoint on the floor above where President Trump, administration officials, members of Congress and journalists were gathered for the dinner. The president and Cabinet members were rushed off the stage after shots were heard in the lobby. No one was hurt during the shooting, with one man arrested in connection with the incident.

Hinckley told TMZ in an interview that he found it “spooky” that the shooting occurred “at the same hotel as mine did.”

He added that the hotel should discontinue holding events “because bad things keep happening” and “it’s just not a secure place to hold big events,” calling security at the hotel “lax.”

Hinckley, then 25, shot Reagan, White House press secretary James Brady, Secret Service agent Timothy McCarthy and Washington, D.C., Metropolitan Police Officer Thomas Delahanty on March 30, 1981. Reagan had spoken at a luncheon while meeting with union members of the AFL-CIO, ending his speech with: “Together, we’ll make America great again.”

Reagan was leaving the hotel when Hinckley opened fire. The former president underwent surgery, while Brady had brain damage and was paralyzed. Brady died from his injuries in 2014.

Hinckley, who believed assassinating the president would win the affection of actress Jodie Foster, was found not guilty of any charges by reason of insanity in 1982. He was allowed to leave a mental health facility in 2016, and a federal judge gave him unconditional release in 2022, shortly after apologizing to the Reagan and Brady families and to Foster.

On Monday, California resident Cole Allen, 31, was charged in federal court with attempting to assassinate Trump, transporting a firearm or ammunition in interstate commerce with the intent to commit a felony and discharge of a firearm during a crime of violence. 

Trump told reporters at the White House mere hours after the shooting that it highlighted the need for a ballroom at the White House. The project has faced lawsuits and criticism since the East Wing was demolished to make way for the ballroom in October.

The event on Saturday marked the first White House Correspondents’ Association dinner that Trump has attended between his two terms, having boycotted previous dinners. He later expressed enthusiasm about attending and assured that the event would be rescheduled in the next 30 days.

The shooting was also the latest of several attempted assassinations on Trump’s life, most notably when he was shot in the ear during a campaign rally in Butler, Pa., in July 2024.
